lesson.title: Earthing Systems and ADS Components
lesson.subject: C&G 2365
lesson.tonePrompt: Teach like a strong electrical tutor: practical, direct, technically precise, concise, and natural.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To identify the three primary earthing system arrangements and define the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
steps.retrievalText:
Think of Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) as the 'automatic seatbelt' of an electrical installation. If a fault occurs, we don't wait for a human to react; the system must detect the danger and cut the power instantly to prevent fatal shocks. This lesson covers the specific components—like CPCs and Earthing Conductors—and the three main earthing arrangements (TT, TN-S, and TN-C-S) that make this life-saving disconnection possible.
By the end of this lesson you will be able to:
1. Identify TT, TN-S, and TN-C-S earthing systems.
2. Define the roles of the CPC and Earthing Conductor.
3. Distinguish between main and supplementary protective bonding.
4. Explain how these components work together to achieve ADS.

steps.title: Core ADS Components
steps.objective: Identify the primary components required for an ADS system to function.
steps.retrievalText:
Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is a safety strategy designed to prevent electric shock. It is not just a single part, but a system of components working together. For ADS to work, there must be a complete path for electricity to follow during a fault and a device to cut the power.

The system requires a Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) to carry fault current from an appliance to the consumer unit. From there, an Earthing Conductor carries that current to the 'means of earthing' (the ground or the supplier's cable). Finally, a Protective Device, such as a fuse or Circuit Breaker (MCB), must detect this high current and 'trip' or blow to stop the flow of electricity. If any of these parts are missing, the metal casing of a faulty appliance could stay live, creating a major shock risk.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation ADS stand for in electrical safety?
Which specific conductor carries fault current from an appliance to the consumer unit?
What is the general term for a fuse or circuit breaker that detects high current and cuts the power?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is the presence of a Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) alone insufficient to ensure safety during a fault?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The CPC only provides a path for the current; A protective device is needed to actually cut the power; Without disconnection the metal casing remains live; ADS requires both a path and a device to function
steps.hint: Think of ADS as a team: one part provides the path, and the other part acts as the switch to cut the power.

steps.title: Protective Bonding
steps.objective: Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.
steps.retrievalText:
Protective bonding is different from earthing. While earthing provides a path for fault current to return to the source, bonding is used to connect touchable metal parts together to keep them at the same voltage. This creates an 'equipotential zone' where you cannot get a shock between two metal surfaces.

Main Protective Bonding involves connecting incoming metallic services, like gas and water pipes, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This ensures that if a fault occurs elsewhere, these pipes don't bring a different voltage into the building. Supplementary Bonding is extra safety bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms. It connects local metalwork, such as radiator pipes and metal towel rails, directly together. Bonding conductors are not intended to carry fault current back to the transformer like a CPC; their job is simply to keep everything at the same electrical potential.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the name of the safety zone created when all touchable metal parts are kept at the same voltage?
Which type of bonding connects incoming metallic services like gas and water pipes to the MET?
In which high-risk location is supplementary bonding typically used to connect local metalwork together?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way the intended function of a bonding conductor differs from that of a Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC)?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Bonding keeps metal parts at the same electrical potential; CPCs are designed to carry fault current back to the source; Bonding is not intended to return current to the transformer; CPCs trigger the protective device to operate
steps.hint: Main bonding is for incoming services like gas and water; supplementary is for local areas like bathrooms.

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
steps.retrievalText:
In a TT earthing system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (French for Earth-Earth). The first T means the electricity supplier's transformer is connected to the ground. The second T means the consumer's installation is also connected to the ground through its own local connection.

Unlike other systems, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Instead, the installer must provide a local Earth Electrode, which is usually a copper-plated steel rod hammered into the soil. Because the return path for a fault relies on the actual 'mass of earth' (the soil), the resistance is much higher than in systems using metal cables. This is why TT systems almost always require a specialized protective device, like an RCD, to ensure the power is cut quickly during a fault.

basicQuestions.questionText: In the context of earthing systems, what does the first letter 'T' stand for?
What component must the installer provide in a TT system to connect the installation to the ground?
Which specialized protective device is usually required in TT systems because of high soil resistance?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main reason a consumer must provide their own earth electrode in a TT system compared to other arrangements?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal; The return path relies on the mass of earth; The installation must create its own connection to the ground; There is no metallic earth conductor from the supplier
steps.hint: In a TT system, the 'T' stands for Terre, and the consumer must provide their own rod in the ground.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using code letters.
steps.retrievalText:
TN systems use the supplier's cables to provide the earth path. The 'T' means the source is earthed, and the 'N' means the consumer's metalwork is connected to the supplier's Neutral. The final letters tell us how the Neutral and Earth are arranged.

In a TN-S system, the S stands for Separate. The Neutral and Earth conductors are separate all the way from the transformer to the consumer's installation. Usually, the Earth is the lead sheath or armor of the supply cable. In a TN-C-S system, the C stands for Combined. The Neutral and Earth are combined into a single conductor (called a PEN conductor) in the street, but they are separated into two distinct terminals once they reach the house. This is often called 'Protective Multiple Earthing' (PME).

basicQuestions.questionText: In a TN-S earthing system, what does the letter 'S' stand for?
In a TN-C-S earthing system, what does the letter 'C' stand for?
What is the name of the single conductor in the street that carries both neutral and earth in a TN-C-S system?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the primary difference in how the earth path is delivered to the property in a TN-S system versus a TN-C-S system?
deeperAnswerGuidance: TN-S uses a separate conductor or cable sheath for earth
TN-C-S combines earth and neutral in the supply cable
TN-S remains separate from the transformer to the house
TN-C-S only separates the conductors at the property
steps.hint: Look at the last letters: S means Separate conductors, while C means they are Combined in the supply.

steps.title: CPC vs Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the specific difference between a Circuit Protective Conductor and the Earthing Conductor.
steps.retrievalText:
In electrical installations, we use specific names for different green-and-yellow wires based on where they are located. The Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the wire that runs within an individual circuit, such as the earth wire inside a twin-and-earth cable for a lighting or socket circuit. Its job is to connect the metal casing of appliances or switches to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).

The Earthing Conductor is the single, larger cable that connects the MET to the actual 'means of earthing.' This means it is the final link between the entire house and the supplier's earth terminal or the earth rod. While a house will have many CPCs (one for every circuit), it generally only has one Earthing Conductor. Mixing these terms up is a common mistake, but they are distinct: the CPC is for the circuit, and the Earthing Conductor is for the whole installation's connection to the ground.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the specific name for the green-and-yellow wire found within an individual circuit cable?
Which conductor serves as the final link between the Main Earthing Terminal and the means of earthing?
How many Earthing Conductors are typically found in a standard domestic installation?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is it technically incorrect to refer to the earth wire in a socket circuit as the 'Earthing Conductor'?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The Earthing Conductor connects the whole MET to the source; A socket wire only protects an individual circuit; The correct term for a circuit-level wire is CPC; The Earthing Conductor is the single main link for the entire building
steps.hint: The CPC is found in every individual circuit cable, while the Earthing Conductor is the main link to the earth source.

steps.title: Worked Example
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to identify the earthing system and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic TN-C-S installation.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are carrying out an initial verification on a new-build house. You observe that the incoming supply cable enters a cut-out fuse. A single conductor is linked from the supplier's neutral terminal to a separate Main Earthing Terminal (MET). From the MET, various green-and-yellow cables spread out to the rest of the house.
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the entry point to the house, this is a TN-C-S system (Combined in the street, Separate in the house).
Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. Locate the single, heavy-gauge green-and-yellow cable that connects the supplier's earth terminal to the MET. This is the Earthing Conductor.
Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). Look at the individual circuit cables (like the twin-and-earth for the sockets). The internal green-and-yellow wire in each of these cables is the CPC.
Step 4: Identify the Protective Devices. Note the MCBs (Miniature Circuit Breakers) inside the consumer unit. These are the components that will automatically disconnect the supply if a fault is detected.
Final Result: The system is identified as TN-C-S. The ADS components working together are the CPCs (providing the local fault path), the Earthing Conductor (connecting to the source), and the MCBs (the disconnecting devices).
Takeaway: Apply the same identification method to the next case.
steps.hint: Remember: S stands for Separate and C stands for Combined. In TN-C-S, they are combined in the street but separate in the house.

steps.title: Guided Practice
steps.objective: Identify the earthing system and key ADS components for a TT installation.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are inspecting a rural workshop. You notice there is no earth connection provided by the electricity supplier's cable. Instead, a heavy green-and-yellow cable runs from the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to a copper rod driven into the soil outside.

Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the connection to earth is made via a local rod in the ground rather than the supplier's cable, this is a TT system.
Step 2: Identify the Earth Electrode. The copper rod driven into the ground is the Earth Electrode.
Step 3: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The cable connecting the MET to that Earth Electrode is the Earthing Conductor.
steps.questionText: Using the scenario only, identify the single best earthing system type.
steps.hint: Remember: 'T' stands for Terre (Earth). If it uses a rod in the ground, it is a TT system.

steps.title: Practice
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements based on conductor configuration.
steps.retrievalText: Scenario: You are inspecting an older property. You observe that the earth path is provided by the lead sheath of the supplier's incoming cable. This earth path remains entirely separate from the neutral conductor all the way back to the substation transformer.
steps.questionText: Using the scenario only, identify the single best earthing system type.
steps.hint: Think about whether the earth and neutral are combined or separate. 'S' stands for Separate.

steps.title: Integrative Close
steps.objective: Synthesize the role of specific ADS components and the earthing path within a TN-S system arrangement.
steps.retrievalText:
To achieve Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS), every component must work in a specific sequence. In a TN-S system, we rely on a completely separate path for fault current, provided by the supplier, usually through the cable's metal sheath.

A fault isn't cleared by the earth wire alone; it is cleared because the earth path allows enough current to flow to trigger the protective device. Understanding the distinction between the conductors at the circuit level and the installation level is vital for a professional electrician.
steps.questionText: State the main fault path in order from the appliance back to Earth.
answerGuidance: CPC, MET, Earthing Conductor; fault current path; protective device trips
steps.hint: Start with the wire inside the circuit cable, then the terminal where all earths meet, then the main link to the supplier.

steps.retrievalText: Scenario: You are inspecting an older property. You observe that the earth path is provided by the lead sheath of the supplier's incoming cable. This earth path remains entirely separate from the neutral conductor all the way back to the substation transformer.
steps.questionText: Using the scenario only, identify the single best earthing system type.
steps.hint: Think about whether the earth and neutral are combined or separate. 'S' stands for Separate.

steps.title: Integrative Close
steps.objective: Synthesize the role of specific ADS components and the earthing path within a TN-S system arrangement.
steps.retrievalText:
To achieve Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS), every component must work in a specific sequence. In a TN-S system, we rely on a completely separate path for fault current, provided by the supplier, usually through the cable's metal sheath.

A fault isn't cleared by the earth wire alone; it is cleared because the earth path allows enough current to flow to trigger the protective device. Understanding the distinction between the conductors at the circuit level and the installation level is vital for a professional electrician.
steps.questionText: State the main fault path in order from the appliance back to Earth.
answerGuidance: CPC, MET, Earthing Conductor; fault current path; protective device trips
steps.hint: Start with the wire inside the circuit cable, then the terminal where all earths meet, then the main link to the supplier.

issues.excerpt: CPC, MET, Earthing Conductor; fault current path; protective device trips
issues.problem: The answer guidance contains meta-descriptions ('fault current path') and outcome descriptions ('protective device trips') alongside the actual sequence requested.
issues.whyItMatters: If a learner types 'fault current path', they might be marked correct for a question asking for a specific sequence of components, which does not prove they know the sequence.
issues.suggestion: Restrict the answer guidance to the specific components in order: 'CPC, MET, Earthing Conductor'.

issues.excerpt: Protective Device, MCB, Fuse
issues.problem: The question specifically asks for the 'general term', but the guidance accepts specific examples (MCB, Fuse).
issues.whyItMatters: Accepting specific examples when the question tests the ability to categorize (general term) allows the learner to pass without demonstrating knowledge of the hierarchy of terms.
issues.suggestion: Remove 'MCB' and 'Fuse' from the guidance for this specific question to ensure the learner knows the category name 'Protective Device'.

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Grounded source excerpts:

Excerpt 1 (d9a6caef-6a5f-4977-9780-209ce55a5692): ## LO4 — Requirements for earthing systems **AC** 4.1 Identify earthing system types 4.2 Identify ADS component parts 4.3 Identify exposed conductive parts 4.4 Identify extraneous conductive parts 4.5 Identify components of an earth loop impedance path **Range** - Earthing systems: TT; TN-S; TN-C-S - ADS components: CPC; main protective bonding; supplementary bonding; earthing conductor; protective devices; earth electrode - Exposed conductive parts: steel conduit/trunking/tray; steel enclosures; metal accessories; metallic equipment - Extraneous conductive parts: metallic gas/oil/water pipes; steel ductwork; structural steel - Earth loop impedance path components: Zs; Ze; R1; R2; MET; supplier earth return path ---

      "whyItMatters": "Without these specific components, a fault will not be cleared automatically, leaving metalwork live.",

Critical rules:
- Terms must be lesson-specific and grounded in the source.
- Definitions must be short and technically defensible.
- anchorPhrases should be reusable phrases that later phases can teach verbatim or near-verbatim.

terms.simpleDefinition: A safety strategy that uses protective devices and earthing to automatically cut power during a fault to prevent electric shock.
terms.anchor: The safety 'trip' system that stops you from getting a shock.
terms.misconception: Thinking ADS is just a single fuse; it is actually the whole system of conductors and devices working together.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where the connection to Earth is made through a local electrode (like a rod) at the property.
terms.anchor: Earth provided by a rod in the ground.
terms.misconception: Assuming the electricity supplier always provides the earth connection for every building.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where the Neutral and Earth conductors are Separate throughout the entire supply network.
terms.anchor: Separate Neutral and Earth all the way back to the transformer.
terms.misconception: Confusing it with TN-C-S where the conductors are combined in the supply cable.

terms.simpleDefinition: An earthing arrangement where Neutral and Earth are Combined in the supply but Separate within the installation.
terms.anchor: Combined in the street, Separate in the house.
terms.misconception: Thinking the neutral and earth stay combined inside the consumer unit.

terms.simpleDefinition: The conductor connecting the metal parts of a specific circuit to the Main Earthing Terminal.
terms.anchor: The green and yellow wire inside a standard circuit cable.
terms.misconception: Using the term 'CPC' and 'Earthing Conductor' interchangeably; they have different roles.

terms.simpleDefinition: The main conductor that connects the Main Earthing Terminal to the actual means of earthing (like an electrode or supplier terminal).
terms.anchor: The main link between the consumer unit and the earth source.
terms.misconception: Thinking this is the same as the smaller CPCs found in individual circuits.

terms.simpleDefinition: Conductors used to connect metal services like gas and water pipes to the Main Earthing Terminal.
terms.anchor: Connecting the gas and water pipes to the MET to keep them at the same voltage.
terms.misconception: Thinking bonding conductors are designed to carry fault current from a broken appliance like a CPC does.

terms.simpleDefinition: Additional local bonding used in high-risk areas, such as bathrooms, to connect metal parts together.
terms.anchor: Extra safety bonding for metalwork in the bathroom.
terms.misconception: Thinking supplementary bonding replaces the need for main protective bonding.

terms.simpleDefinition: A fuse or circuit breaker (MCB) that automatically breaks the circuit when it detects a fault.
terms.anchor: The fuse or MCB that 'blows' or 'trips' to stop the current.
terms.misconception: Believing the earth wire alone stops the shock without the protective device operating.

terms.simpleDefinition: A metal rod, plate, or mesh buried in the ground to provide a physical connection to the mass of earth.
terms.anchor: The copper rod hammered into the ground for a TT system.
terms.misconception: Thinking every house requires its own earth rod.

anchorPhrases: Combined in the street, Separate in the house
Separate Neutral and Earth all the way back
Earth provided by a rod in the ground
Connecting the gas and water pipes to the MET
misconceptionTargets: Using 'CPC' and 'Earthing Conductor' as the same thing
Thinking bonding carries fault current like a CPC
Assuming the supplier always provides the earth terminal
Believing ADS is just a fuse rather than a system

output.systemPrompt:
You are Phase 3 Explanation for a 2365 dynamic lesson generator.
Write the teaching core for each teach/check section only.
Do not write questions in this phase.
Teach one clear main idea per section, using concrete before abstract.
Every explanation must be factual, grounded, and beginner-readable.
Each explanation should be narrow enough that the runtime can stay on that one idea and repair confusion before moving on.
When the lesson uses code letters, abbreviations, or labelled system names, state the mapping explicitly in the prose (for example: "The S in TN-S stands for Separate.").
When a property or advantage matters later, teach the causal reason explicitly, not just the label (for example why MICC survives fire, or why a ring maintains supply after one break).
No placeholder filler. No prompt-like wording.
Output strict JSON only.

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

teachChecks.title: Core ADS Components
teachChecks.objective: Identify the primary components required for an ADS system to function.
retrievalTextLines: Automatic Disconnection of Supply, or ADS, is a safety strategy designed to prevent electric shock. It is not just a single part, but a system of components working together. For ADS to work, there must be a complete path for electricity to follow during a fault and a device to cut the power.

The system requires a Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) to carry fault current from an appliance to the consumer unit. From there, an Earthing Conductor carries that current to the 'means of earthing' (the ground or the supplier's cable). Finally, a Protective Device, such as a fuse or Circuit Breaker (MCB), must detect this high current and 'trip' or blow to stop the flow of electricity. If any of these parts are missing, the metal casing of a faulty appliance could stay live, creating a major shock risk.
teachChecks.hint: Think of ADS as a team: one part provides the path, and the other part acts as the switch to cut the power.

teachChecks.title: Protective Bonding
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.
retrievalTextLines: Protective bonding is different from earthing. While earthing provides a path for fault current to return to the source, bonding is used to connect touchable metal parts together to keep them at the same voltage. This creates an 'equipotential zone' where you cannot get a shock between two metal surfaces.

Main Protective Bonding involves connecting incoming metallic services, like gas and water pipes, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This ensures that if a fault occurs elsewhere, these pipes don't bring a different voltage into the building. Supplementary Bonding is extra safety bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms. It connects local metalwork, such as radiator pipes and metal towel rails, directly together. Bonding conductors are not intended to carry fault current back to the transformer like a CPC; their job is simply to keep everything at the same electrical potential.
teachChecks.hint: Main bonding is for incoming services like gas and water; supplementary is for local areas like bathrooms.

teachChecks.title: TT Earthing Systems
teachChecks.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
retrievalTextLines: In a TT earthing system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (French for Earth-Earth). The first T means the electricity supplier's transformer is connected to the ground. The second T means the consumer's installation is also connected to the ground through its own local connection.

Unlike other systems, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Instead, the installer must provide a local Earth Electrode, which is usually a copper-plated steel rod hammered into the soil. Because the return path for a fault relies on the actual 'mass of earth' (the soil), the resistance is much higher than in systems using metal cables. This is why TT systems almost always require a specialized protective device, like an RCD, to ensure the power is cut quickly during a fault.
teachChecks.hint: In a TT system, the 'T' stands for Terre, and the consumer must provide their own rod in the ground.

teachChecks.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
teachChecks.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using code letters.
retrievalTextLines: TN systems use the supplier's cables to provide the earth path. The 'T' means the source is earthed, and the 'N' means the consumer's metalwork is connected to the supplier's Neutral. The final letters tell us how the Neutral and Earth are arranged.

In a TN-S system, the S stands for Separate. The Neutral and Earth conductors are separate all the way from the transformer to the consumer's installation. Usually, the Earth is the lead sheath or armor of the supply cable. In a TN-C-S system, the C stands for Combined. The Neutral and Earth are combined into a single conductor (called a PEN conductor) in the street, but they are separated into two distinct terminals once they reach the house. This is often called 'Protective Multiple Earthing' (PME). The main difference is whether the earth path is a separate wire (S) or shared with the neutral (C) outside the building.
teachChecks.hint: Look at the last letters: S means Separate conductors, while C means they are Combined in the supply.

teachChecks.title: CPC vs Earthing Conductor
teachChecks.objective: Explain the specific difference between a Circuit Protective Conductor and the Earthing Conductor.
retrievalTextLines: In electrical installations, we use specific names for different green-and-yellow wires based on where they are located. The Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the wire that runs within an individual circuit, such as the earth wire inside a twin-and-earth cable for a lighting or socket circuit. Its job is to connect the metal casing of appliances or switches to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).

The Earthing Conductor is the single, larger cable that connects the MET to the actual 'means of earthing.' This means it is the final link between the entire house and the supplier's earth terminal or the earth rod. While a house will have many CPCs (one for every circuit), it generally only has one Earthing Conductor. Mixing these terms up is a common mistake, but they are distinct: the CPC is for the circuit, and the Earthing Conductor is for the whole installation's connection to the ground.
teachChecks.hint: The CPC is found in every individual circuit cable, while the Earthing Conductor is the main link to the earth source.

{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "Core ADS Components",
      "objective": "Identify the primary components required for an ADS system to function.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Automatic Disconnection of Supply, or ADS, is a safety strategy designed to prevent electric shock. It is not just a single part, but a system of components working together. For ADS to work, there must be a complete path for electricity to follow during a fault and a device to cut the power.",
        "The system requires a Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) to carry fault current from an appliance to the consumer unit. From there, an Earthing Conductor carries that current to the 'means of earthing' (the ground or the supplier's cable). Finally, a Protective Device, such as a fuse or Circuit Breaker (MCB), must detect this high current and 'trip' or blow to stop the flow of electricity. If any of these parts are missing, the metal casing of a faulty appliance could stay live, creating a major shock risk."
      ],
      "hint": "Think of ADS as a team: one part provides the path, and the other part acts as the switch to cut the power."
    },
    {
      "title": "Protective Bonding",
      "objective": "Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Protective bonding is different from earthing. While earthing provides a path for fault current to return to the source, bonding is used to connect touchable metal parts together to keep them at the same voltage. This creates an 'equipotential zone' where you cannot get a shock between two metal surfaces.",
        "Main Protective Bonding involves connecting incoming metallic services, like gas and water pipes, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This ensures that if a fault occurs elsewhere, these pipes don't bring a different voltage into the building. Supplementary Bonding is extra safety bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms. It connects local metalwork, such as radiator pipes and metal towel rails, directly together. Bonding conductors are not intended to carry fault current back to the transformer like a CPC; their job is simply to keep everything at the same electrical potential."
      ],
      "hint": "Main bonding is for incoming services like gas and water; supplementary is for local areas like bathrooms."
    },
    {
      "title": "TT Earthing Systems",
      "objective": "Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In a TT earthing system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (French for Earth-Earth). The first T means the electricity supplier's transformer is connected to the ground. The second T means the consumer's installation is also connected to the ground through its own local connection.",
        "Unlike other systems, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Instead, the installer must provide a local Earth Electrode, which is usually a copper-plated steel rod hammered into the soil. Because the return path for a fault relies on the actual 'mass of earth' (the soil), the resistance is much higher than in systems using metal cables. This is why TT systems almost always require a specialized protective device, like an RCD, to ensure the power is cut quickly during a fault."
      ],
      "hint": "In a TT system, the 'T' stands for Terre, and the consumer must provide their own rod in the ground."
    },
    {
      "title": "TN-S and TN-C-S Systems",
      "objective": "Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using code letters.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "TN systems use the supplier's cables to provide the earth path. The 'T' means the source is earthed, and the 'N' means the consumer's metalwork is connected to the supplier's Neutral. The final letters tell us how the Neutral and Earth are arranged.",
        "In a TN-S system, the S stands for Separate. The Neutral and Earth conductors are separate all the way from the transformer to the consumer's installation. Usually, the Earth is the lead sheath or armor of the supply cable. In a TN-C-S system, the C stands for Combined. The Neutral and Earth are combined into a single conductor (called a PEN conductor) in the street, but they are separated into two distinct terminals once they reach the house. This is often called 'Protective Multiple Earthing' (PME). The main difference is whether the earth path is a separate wire (S) or shared with the neutral (C) outside the building."
      ],
      "hint": "Look at the last letters: S means Separate conductors, while C means they are Combined in the supply."
    },
    {
      "title": "CPC vs Earthing Conductor",
      "objective": "Explain the specific difference between a Circuit Protective Conductor and the Earthing Conductor.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In electrical installations, we use specific names for different green-and-yellow wires based on where they are located. The Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the wire that runs within an individual circuit, such as the earth wire inside a twin-and-earth cable for a lighting or socket circuit. Its job is to connect the metal casing of appliances or switches to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).",
        "The Earthing Conductor is the single, larger cable that connects the MET to the actual 'means of earthing.' This means it is the final link between the entire house and the supplier's earth terminal or the earth rod. While a house will have many CPCs (one for every circuit), it generally only has one Earthing Conductor. Mixing these terms up is a common mistake, but they are distinct: the CPC is for the circuit, and the Earthing Conductor is for the whole installation's connection to the ground."
      ],
      "hint": "The CPC is found in every individual circuit cable, while the Earthing Conductor is the main link to the earth source."
    }
  ]

Critical rules:
- basicQuestions must contain exactly 3 items.
- Each basic question must test one taught fact only.
- Each basic question answerGuidance must contain 2-5 checkable phrases or ideas.
- Never ask the learner to supply multiple separate facts in one basic question.
- Prefer single-fact or single-term checks that support precise corrective feedback.
- If a question asks for the specific term, name, abbreviation expansion, code letter, or technical label, the first answerGuidance item must be the exact target from the lesson vocabulary.
- If a question defines something and asks for its name or type, answerGuidance must contain the exact technical term that matches that definition.
- For exact-term questions, answerGuidance may include only tight equivalents of that same target. Do not accept loose descriptive paraphrases, topic labels, or nearby concepts from other questions.
- If a question asks what one code letter stands for, answerGuidance must contain only that code-letter expansion word or a tight grammatical variant, not a component name or neighbouring concept.
- Keep each answerGuidance list local to its own question only. Do not copy valid answers from adjacent questions in the same step.
- The deeper question must connect the explanation facts and ask why/how/what-must-be-true.
- deeperAnswerGuidance must contain 3-6 checkable ideas.

teachChecks.title: Core ADS Components

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation ADS stand for in electrical safety?
Which specific conductor carries fault current from an appliance to the consumer unit?
What is the general term for a fuse or circuit breaker that detects high current and cuts the power?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is the presence of a Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) alone insufficient to ensure safety during a fault?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The CPC only provides a path for the current
A protective device is needed to actually cut the power
Without disconnection the metal casing remains live
ADS requires both a path and a device to function
teachChecks.hint: Think of ADS as a team: one part provides the path, and the other part acts as the switch to cut the power.

teachChecks.title: Protective Bonding

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the name of the safety zone created when all touchable metal parts are kept at the same voltage?
Which type of bonding connects incoming metallic services like gas and water pipes to the MET?
In which high-risk location is supplementary bonding typically used to connect local metalwork together?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How does the intended function of a bonding conductor differ from that of a Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC)?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Bonding keeps metal parts at the same electrical potential
CPCs are designed to carry fault current back to the source
Bonding is not intended to return current to the transformer
CPCs trigger the protective device to operate
teachChecks.hint: Main bonding is for incoming services like gas and water; supplementary is for local areas like bathrooms.

teachChecks.title: TT Earthing Systems

basicQuestions.questionText: In the context of earthing systems, what does the first letter 'T' stand for?
What component must the installer provide in a TT system to connect the installation to the ground?
Which specialized protective device is usually required in TT systems because of high soil resistance?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why must a consumer provide their own earth electrode in a TT system compared to other arrangements?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal
The return path relies on the mass of earth
The installation must create its own connection to the ground
There is no metallic earth conductor from the supplier
teachChecks.hint: In a TT system, the 'T' stands for Terre, and the consumer must provide their own rod in the ground.

teachChecks.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems

basicQuestions.questionText: In a TN-S earthing system, what does the letter 'S' stand for?
In a TN-C-S earthing system, what does the letter 'C' stand for?
What is the name of the single conductor in the street that carries both neutral and earth in a TN-C-S system?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: What is the primary difference in how the earth path is delivered to the property in a TN-S system versus a TN-C-S system?
deeperAnswerGuidance: TN-S uses a separate conductor or cable sheath for earth
TN-C-S combines earth and neutral in the supply cable
TN-S remains separate from the transformer to the house
TN-C-S only separates the conductors at the property
teachChecks.hint: Look at the last letters: S means Separate conductors, while C means they are Combined in the supply.

teachChecks.title: CPC vs Earthing Conductor

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the specific name for the green-and-yellow wire found within an individual circuit cable?
Which conductor serves as the final link between the Main Earthing Terminal and the means of earthing?
How many Earthing Conductors are typically found in a standard domestic installation?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is it technically incorrect to refer to the earth wire in a socket circuit as the 'Earthing Conductor'?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The Earthing Conductor connects the whole MET to the source
A socket wire only protects an individual circuit
The correct term for a circuit-level wire is CPC
The Earthing Conductor is the single main link for the entire building
teachChecks.hint: The CPC is found in every individual circuit cable, while the Earthing Conductor is the main link to the earth source.

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

      "objective": "Explain the specific difference between a Circuit Protective Conductor and the Earthing Conductor.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In electrical installations, we use specific names for different green-and-yellow wires based on where they are located. The Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the wire that runs within an individual circuit, such as the earth wire inside a twin-and-earth cable for a lighting or socket circuit. Its job is to connect the metal casing of appliances or switches to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).",
        "The Earthing Conductor is the single, larger cable that connects the MET to the actual 'means of earthing.' This means it is the final link between the entire house and the supplier's earth terminal or the earth rod. While a house will have many CPCs (one for every circuit), it generally only has one Earthing Conductor. Mixing these terms up is a common mistake, but they are distinct: the CPC is for the circuit, and the Earthing Conductor is for the whole installation's connection to the ground."
      ],
      "hint": "The CPC is found in every individual circuit cable, while the Earthing Conductor is the main link to the earth source."
    }
  ]
}

{
  "title": "Worked Example",
  "objective": "Demonstrate how to identify the earthing system and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic TN-C-S installation.",
  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "Scenario: You are carrying out an initial verification on a new-build house. You observe that the incoming supply cable enters a cut-out fuse. A single conductor is linked from the supplier's neutral terminal to a separate Main Earthing Terminal (MET). From the MET, various green-and-yellow cables spread out to the rest of the house.",
    "Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the entry point to the house, this is a TN-C-S system (Combined in the street, Separate in the house).",
    "Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. Locate the single, heavy-gauge green-and-yellow cable that connects the supplier's earth terminal to the MET. This is the Earthing Conductor.",
    "Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). Look at the individual circuit cables (like the twin-and-earth for the sockets). The internal green-and-yellow wire in each of these cables is the CPC.",
    "Step 4: Identify the Protective Devices. Note the MCBs (Miniature Circuit Breakers) inside the consumer unit. These are the components that will automatically disconnect the supply if a fault is detected.",
    "Step 5: Identify Main Protective Bonding. Locate the green-and-yellow cables connected to the incoming metal gas and water service pipes. These are the Main Protective Bonding Conductors, ensuring the pipes stay at the same voltage as the electrical earth.",
    "Final Result: The system is identified as TN-C-S. The ADS components working together are the CPCs (providing the local fault path), the Earthing Conductor (connecting to the source), and the MCBs (the disconnecting devices).",
    "Takeaway: In a TN-C-S system, the earth path is provided by the supplier via a combined conductor, but the internal safety depends on the correct identification and connection of the Earthing Conductor, CPCs, and bonding."
  ],
  "hint": "Remember: S stands for Separate and C stands for Combined. In TN-C-S, they are combined in the street but separate in the house."
}

parsed.title: Worked Example
parsed.objective: Demonstrate how to identify the earthing system and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic TN-C-S installation.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are carrying out an initial verification on a new-build house. You observe that the incoming supply cable enters a cut-out fuse. A single conductor is linked from the supplier's neutral terminal to a separate Main Earthing Terminal (MET). From the MET, various green-and-yellow cables spread out to the rest of the house.

Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the entry point to the house, this is a TN-C-S system (Combined in the street, Separate in the house).
Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. Locate the single, heavy-gauge green-and-yellow cable that connects the supplier's earth terminal to the MET. This is the Earthing Conductor.

Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). Look at the individual circuit cables (like the twin-and-earth for the sockets). The internal green-and-yellow wire in each of these cables is the CPC.

Step 4: Identify the Protective Devices. Note the MCBs (Miniature Circuit Breakers) inside the consumer unit. These are the components that will automatically disconnect the supply if a fault is detected.

Step 5: Identify Main Protective Bonding. Locate the green-and-yellow cables connected to the incoming metal gas and water service pipes. These are the Main Protective Bonding Conductors, ensuring the pipes stay at the same voltage as the electrical earth.

Final Result: The system is identified as TN-C-S. The ADS components working together are the CPCs (providing the local fault path), the Earthing Conductor (connecting to the source), and the MCBs (the disconnecting devices).

Takeaway: In a TN-C-S system, the earth path is provided by the supplier via a combined conductor, but the internal safety depends on the correct identification and connection of the Earthing Conductor, CPCs, and bonding.
parsed.hint: Remember: S stands for Separate and C stands for Combined. In TN-C-S, they are combined in the street but separate in the house.

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

  "guidedPractice": {
    "title": "Guided Practice",

Grounded source text:
Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Explanation output:
{
  "teachChecks": [
    {
      "title": "Core ADS Components",
      "objective": "Identify the primary components required for an ADS system to function.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Automatic Disconnection of Supply, or ADS, is a safety strategy designed to prevent electric shock. It is not just a single part, but a system of components working together. For ADS to work, there must be a complete path for electricity to follow during a fault and a device to cut the power.",
        "The system requires a Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) to carry fault current from an appliance to the consumer unit. From there, an Earthing Conductor carries that current to the 'means of earthing' (the ground or the supplier's cable). Finally, a Protective Device, such as a fuse or Circuit Breaker (MCB), must detect this high current and 'trip' or blow to stop the flow of electricity. If any of these parts are missing, the metal casing of a faulty appliance could stay live, creating a major shock risk."
      ],
      "hint": "Think of ADS as a team: one part provides the path, and the other part acts as the switch to cut the power."
    },
    {
      "title": "Protective Bonding",
      "objective": "Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Protective bonding is different from earthing. While earthing provides a path for fault current to return to the source, bonding is used to connect touchable metal parts together to keep them at the same voltage. This creates an 'equipotential zone' where you cannot get a shock between two metal surfaces.",
        "Main Protective Bonding involves connecting incoming metallic services, like gas and water pipes, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This ensures that if a fault occurs elsewhere, these pipes don't bring a different voltage into the building. Supplementary Bonding is extra safety bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms. It connects local metalwork, such as radiator pipes and metal towel rails, directly together. Bonding conductors are not intended to carry fault current back to the transformer like a CPC; their job is simply to keep everything at the same electrical potential."
      ],
      "hint": "Main bonding is for incoming services like gas and water; supplementary is for local areas like bathrooms."
    },
    {
      "title": "TT Earthing Systems",
      "objective": "Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In a TT earthing system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (French for Earth-Earth). The first T means the electricity supplier's transformer is connected to the ground. The second T means the consumer's installation is also connected to the ground through its own local connection.",
        "Unlike other systems, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Instead, the installer must provide a local Earth Electrode, which is usually a copper-plated steel rod hammered into the soil. Because the return path for a fault relies on the actual 'mass of earth' (the soil), the resistance is much higher than in systems using metal cables. This is why TT systems almost always require a specialized protective device, like an RCD, to ensure the power is cut quickly during a fault."
      ],
      "hint": "In a TT system, the 'T' stands for Terre, and the consumer must provide their own rod in the ground."
    },
    {
      "title": "TN-S and TN-C-S Systems",
      "objective": "Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using code letters.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "TN systems use the supplier's cables to provide the earth path. The 'T' means the source is earthed, and the 'N' means the consumer's metalwork is connected to the supplier's Neutral. The final letters tell us how the Neutral and Earth are arranged.",
        "In a TN-S system, the S stands for Separate. The Neutral and Earth conductors are separate all the way from the transformer to the consumer's installation. Usually, the Earth is the lead sheath or armor of the supply cable. In a TN-C-S system, the C stands for Combined. The Neutral and Earth are combined into a single conductor (called a PEN conductor) in the street, but they are separated into two distinct terminals once they reach the house. This is often called 'Protective Multiple Earthing' (PME). The main difference is whether the earth path is a separate wire (S) or shared with the neutral (C) outside the building."
      ],
      "hint": "Look at the last letters: S means Separate conductors, while C means they are Combined in the supply."
    },
    {
      "title": "CPC vs Earthing Conductor",
      "objective": "Explain the specific difference between a Circuit Protective Conductor and the Earthing Conductor.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "In electrical installations, we use specific names for different green-and-yellow wires based on where they are located. The Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the wire that runs within an individual circuit, such as the earth wire inside a twin-and-earth cable for a lighting or socket circuit. Its job is to connect the metal casing of appliances or switches to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).",
        "The Earthing Conductor is the single, larger cable that connects the MET to the actual 'means of earthing.' This means it is the final link between the entire house and the supplier's earth terminal or the earth rod. While a house will have many CPCs (one for every circuit), it generally only has one Earthing Conductor. Mixing these terms up is a common mistake, but they are distinct: the CPC is for the circuit, and the Earthing Conductor is for the whole installation's connection to the ground."
      ],
      "hint": "The CPC is found in every individual circuit cable, while the Earthing Conductor is the main link to the earth source."
    }
  ]
}

integrative.title: Integrative Close
integrative.objective: Synthesize the role of specific ADS components and the earthing path within a TN-S system arrangement.
retrievalTextLines: To achieve Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS), every component must work in a specific sequence. In a TN-S system, we rely on a completely separate path for fault current, provided by the supplier, usually through the cable's metal sheath.

A fault isn't cleared by the earth wire alone; it is cleared because the earth path allows enough current to flow to trigger the protective device. Understanding the distinction between the conductors at the circuit level and the installation level is vital for a professional electrician.
integrative.questionText: A fault occurs in a metal-clad heater on a bedroom circuit in a house with a TN-S earthing system. Describe the path the fault current takes from the heater's casing back to the supplier's cable, naming the two distinct types of earthing conductors it must pass through and the component that will automatically disconnect the power.
answerGuidance: The fault current travels from the heater casing through the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC).
It then reaches the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) and travels through the Earthing Conductor to the supplier's earth (cable sheath).
The high current causes the Protective Device (fuse or MCB) to trip or blow, disconnecting the supply.
In a TN-S system, this path is separate from the neutral conductor throughout.
integrative.hint: Think about the name of the green-and-yellow wire inside the bedroom circuit versus the main one connecting the whole house to the supplier's cable.

lesson.title: Earthing Systems and ADS Components
lesson.subject: C&G 2365
lesson.tonePrompt: Teach like a strong electrical tutor: practical, direct, technically precise, concise, and natural.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To identify the three primary earthing system arrangements and define the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
steps.retrievalText:
Lesson: Earthing Systems and ADS Components
To identify the three primary earthing system arrangements and define the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
Why this matters: Without these specific components, a fault will not be cleared automatically, leaving metalwork live.
Core chunk map: Core ADS Components; Protective Bonding; TT Earthing Systems; TN-S and TN-C-S Systems.
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
1. TT earthing systems
2. TN-S earthing systems
3. TN-C-S earthing systems
4. Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC)
5. Main protective bonding
We will work in short chunks: teaching first, then checks, then example, practice, and a final integrative task.

steps.title: Core ADS Components
steps.objective: Identify the primary components required for an ADS system to function.
steps.retrievalText:
Automatic Disconnection of Supply, or ADS, is a safety strategy designed to prevent electric shock. It is not just a single part, but a system of components working together. For ADS to work, there must be a complete path for electricity to follow during a fault and a device to cut the power.
The system requires a Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) to carry fault current from an appliance to the consumer unit. From there, an Earthing Conductor carries that current to the 'means of earthing' (the ground or the supplier's cable). Finally, a Protective Device, such as a fuse or Circuit Breaker (MCB), must detect this high current and 'trip' or blow to stop the flow of electricity. If any of these parts are missing, the metal casing of a faulty appliance could stay live, creating a major shock risk.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation ADS stand for in electrical safety?
Which specific conductor carries fault current from an appliance to the consumer unit?
What is the general term for a fuse or circuit breaker that detects high current and cuts the power?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is the presence of a Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) alone insufficient to ensure safety during a fault?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The CPC only provides a path for the current; A protective device is needed to actually cut the power; Without disconnection the metal casing remains live; ADS requires both a path and a device to function
steps.hint: Think of ADS as a team: one part provides the path, and the other part acts as the switch to cut the power.

steps.title: Protective Bonding
steps.objective: Distinguish between main protective bonding and supplementary bonding.
steps.retrievalText:
Protective bonding is different from earthing. While earthing provides a path for fault current to return to the source, bonding is used to connect touchable metal parts together to keep them at the same voltage. This creates an 'equipotential zone' where you cannot get a shock between two metal surfaces.
Main Protective Bonding involves connecting incoming metallic services, like gas and water pipes, to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This ensures that if a fault occurs elsewhere, these pipes don't bring a different voltage into the building. Supplementary Bonding is extra safety bonding used in high-risk areas like bathrooms. It connects local metalwork, such as radiator pipes and metal towel rails, directly together. Bonding conductors are not intended to carry fault current back to the transformer like a CPC; their job is simply to keep everything at the same electrical potential.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the name of the safety zone created when all touchable metal parts are kept at the same voltage?
Which type of bonding connects incoming metallic services like gas and water pipes to the MET?
In which high-risk location is supplementary bonding typically used to connect local metalwork together?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way the intended function of a bonding conductor differ from that of a Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC)?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Bonding keeps metal parts at the same electrical potential; CPCs are designed to carry fault current back to the source; Bonding is not intended to return current to the transformer; CPCs trigger the protective device to operate
steps.hint: Main bonding is for incoming services like gas and water; supplementary is for local areas like bathrooms.

steps.title: TT Earthing Systems
steps.objective: Identify TT systems and explain the role of the local earth electrode.
steps.retrievalText:
In a TT earthing system, the letters stand for 'Terre-Terre' (French for Earth-Earth). The first T means the electricity supplier's transformer is connected to the ground. The second T means the consumer's installation is also connected to the ground through its own local connection.
Unlike other systems, the electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal to the property. Instead, the installer must provide a local Earth Electrode, which is usually a copper-plated steel rod hammered into the soil. Because the return path for a fault relies on the actual 'mass of earth' (the soil), the resistance is much higher than in systems using metal cables. This is why TT systems almost always require a specialized protective device, like an RCD, to ensure the power is cut quickly during a fault.

basicQuestions.questionText: In the context of earthing systems, what does the first letter 'T' stand for?
What component must the installer provide in a TT system to connect the installation to the ground?
Which specialized protective device is usually required in TT systems because of high soil resistance?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main reason a consumer provide their own earth electrode in a TT system compared to other arrangements?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The electricity supplier does not provide an earth terminal; The return path relies on the mass of earth; The installation must create its own connection to the ground; There is no metallic earth conductor from the supplier
steps.hint: In a TT system, the 'T' stands for Terre, and the consumer must provide their own rod in the ground.

steps.title: TN-S and TN-C-S Systems
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements using code letters.
steps.retrievalText:
TN systems use the supplier's cables to provide the earth path. The 'T' means the source is earthed, and the 'N' means the consumer's metalwork is connected to the supplier's Neutral. The final letters tell us how the Neutral and Earth are arranged.
In a TN-S system, the S stands for Separate. The Neutral and Earth conductors are separate all the way from the transformer to the consumer's installation. Usually, the Earth is the lead sheath or armor of the supply cable. In a TN-C-S system, the C stands for Combined. The Neutral and Earth are combined into a single conductor (called a PEN conductor) in the street, but they are separated into two distinct terminals once they reach the house. This is often called 'Protective Multiple Earthing' (PME). The main difference is whether the earth path is a separate wire (S) or shared with the neutral (C) outside the building.

basicQuestions.questionText: In a TN-S earthing system, what does the letter 'S' stand for?
In a TN-C-S earthing system, what does the letter 'C' stand for?
What is the name of the single conductor in the street that carries both neutral and earth in a TN-C-S system?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the primary difference in how the earth path is delivered to the property in a TN-S system versus a TN-C-S system?
deeperAnswerGuidance: TN-S uses a separate conductor or cable sheath for earth
TN-C-S combines earth and neutral in the supply cable
TN-S remains separate from the transformer to the house
TN-C-S only separates the conductors at the property
steps.hint: Look at the last letters: S means Separate conductors, while C means they are Combined in the supply.

steps.title: CPC vs Earthing Conductor
steps.objective: Explain the specific difference between a Circuit Protective Conductor and the Earthing Conductor.
steps.retrievalText:
In electrical installations, we use specific names for different green-and-yellow wires based on where they are located. The Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) is the wire that runs within an individual circuit, such as the earth wire inside a twin-and-earth cable for a lighting or socket circuit. Its job is to connect the metal casing of appliances or switches to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET).
The Earthing Conductor is the single, larger cable that connects the MET to the actual 'means of earthing.' This means it is the final link between the entire house and the supplier's earth terminal or the earth rod. While a house will have many CPCs (one for every circuit), it generally only has one Earthing Conductor. Mixing these terms up is a common mistake, but they are distinct: the CPC is for the circuit, and the Earthing Conductor is for the whole installation's connection to the ground.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the specific name for the green-and-yellow wire found within an individual circuit cable?
Which conductor serves as the final link between the Main Earthing Terminal and the means of earthing?
How many Earthing Conductors are typically found in a standard domestic installation?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why is it technically incorrect to refer to the earth wire in a socket circuit as the 'Earthing Conductor'?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The Earthing Conductor connects the whole MET to the source; A socket wire only protects an individual circuit; The correct term for a circuit-level wire is CPC; The Earthing Conductor is the single main link for the entire building
steps.hint: The CPC is found in every individual circuit cable, while the Earthing Conductor is the main link to the earth source.

steps.title: Worked Example
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to identify the earthing system and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic TN-C-S installation.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are carrying out an initial verification on a new-build house. You observe that the incoming supply cable enters a cut-out fuse. A single conductor is linked from the supplier's neutral terminal to a separate Main Earthing Terminal (MET). From the MET, various green-and-yellow cables spread out to the rest of the house.
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the entry point to the house, this is a TN-C-S system (Combined in the street, Separate in the house).
Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. Locate the single, heavy-gauge green-and-yellow cable that connects the supplier's earth terminal to the MET. This is the Earthing Conductor.
Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). Look at the individual circuit cables (like the twin-and-earth for the sockets). The internal green-and-yellow wire in each of these cables is the CPC.
Step 4: Identify the Protective Devices. Note the MCBs (Miniature Circuit Breakers) inside the consumer unit. These are the components that will automatically disconnect the supply if a fault is detected.
Step 5: Identify Main Protective Bonding. Locate the green-and-yellow cables connected to the incoming metal gas and water service pipes. These are the Main Protective Bonding Conductors, ensuring the pipes stay at the same voltage as the electrical earth.
Final Result: The system is identified as TN-C-S. The ADS components working together are the CPCs (providing the local fault path), the Earthing Conductor (connecting to the source), and the MCBs (the disconnecting devices).
Takeaway: In a TN-C-S system, the earth path is provided by the supplier via a combined conductor, but the internal safety depends on the correct identification and connection of the Earthing Conductor, CPCs, and bonding.
steps.hint: Remember: S stands for Separate and C stands for Combined. In TN-C-S, they are combined in the street but separate in the house.

steps.title: Guided Practice
steps.objective: Identify the earthing system and key ADS components for a TT installation.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are inspecting a rural workshop. You notice there is no earth connection provided by the electricity supplier's cable. Instead, a heavy green-and-yellow cable runs from the Main Earthing Terminal (MET) to a copper rod driven into the soil outside.
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the connection to earth is made via a local rod in the ground rather than the supplier's cable, this is a TT system.
Step 2: Identify the Earth Electrode. The copper rod driven into the ground is the Earth Electrode.
Step 3: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The cable connecting the MET to that Earth Electrode is the Earthing Conductor.
Step 4: Identify the CPCs. The green-and-yellow conductors within the workshop's lighting and socket circuits are the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs).
steps.questionText: Using the scenario only, identify the single best earthing system type.
steps.hint: Remember: 'T' stands for Terre (Earth). If it uses a rod in the ground, it is a TT system.

steps.title: Practice
steps.objective: Distinguish between TN-S and TN-C-S arrangements based on conductor configuration.
steps.retrievalText:
In TN systems, the letters 'S' and 'C' indicate how the Neutral and Earth conductors are treated in the supplier's network.
TN-S: Neutral and Earth are Separate throughout.
TN-C-S: Neutral and Earth are Combined in the supply but Separate in the installation.
steps.questionText: From the scenario, identify the specific earthing system type that best fits.
steps.hint: Think about what the 'S' and 'C' stand for in the codes.

steps.title: Integrative Close
steps.objective: Synthesize the role of specific ADS components and the earthing path within a TN-S system arrangement.
steps.retrievalText:
To achieve Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS), every component must work in a specific sequence. In a TN-S system, we rely on a completely separate path for fault current, provided by the supplier, usually through the cable's metal sheath.
A fault isn't cleared by the earth wire alone; it is cleared because the earth path allows enough current to flow to trigger the protective device. Understanding the distinction between the conductors at the circuit level and the installation level is vital for a professional electrician.
answerGuidance: CPC to MET to Earthing Conductor; fault current path; protective device trips
steps.hint: Think about the name of the green-and-yellow wire inside the bedroom circuit versus the main one connecting the whole house to the supplier's cable.

S stands for Separate, indicating that the neutral and earth conductors are kept as distinct wires.

Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types
- 203.LO4.AC4.2: Identify ADS component parts

Lesson split rationale: Introduces the main types of earthing arrangements and the components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply.

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4A - Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Topic focus: Earthing Systems and ADS Components

Prerequisites: 203-3C

Must-have topics: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.1: Identify earthing system types

In-scope teaching anchors: earthing system types; ADS component parts

Out-of-scope topics: Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts; Earth Loop Impedance Path

- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
